action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before, and you
have NO examples to start from. Your task is to work out how it works entirely on your
own, and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: bird, stone, hill, boat, dog, river, farmer, tree, king, spear, hunter, road, ship, lake
Adjectives: tall, small, old, red, dark, big, blue, cold
Verbs (write them in this base form): chase, hear, watch, feed, hold, find, see, carry
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a bird chase a stone
    six birds chase a stone
    a tall bird chase a stone

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. You are starting from nothing, so spend your early probes establishing the
   basic words and word order, then test how number, case, definiteness, agreement and
   any sound changes work. Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a bird chase a stone
2. the king hold a spear
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird chase a stone"  =>  ketoge lupo pepa
2. "the king hold a spear"  =>  patalo bogala galu la
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 2 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two translations I can infer the language has a verb “hold” (patalo) and a noun “spear” (galu). The first sentence “a bird chase a stone” maps to “ketoge lupo pepa”, so the verb “chase” is likely “lupo” and the subject “a bird” is “ketoge”. The second sentence “the king hold a spear” maps to “patalo bogala galu la”, so “patalo” is the verb “hold”, and “bogala” is the object “spear”. I also see a definite article marker “la” at the end of the second clause, suggesting a definite article “the” is marked by “la”. I’ll probe the verb “chase” and the noun “bird”, and test number/quantity with “two” and “three”.
PROBES:
1. two birds chase a stone
2. three birds chase a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two birds chase a stone"  =>  ketoge lupo te pepahu
2. "three birds chase a stone"  =>  ketoge lupo tu pepahu
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The verb “chase” is consistently “lupo”. The subject “a bird” is “ketoge”. The object “a stone” is “pepa”. With two birds it becomes “ketoge lupo te pepahu” (additive marker “te”), and with three birds it becomes “ketoge lupo tu pepahu” (additive marker “tu”). So “te” = “two” and “tu” = “three”. I’ll keep testing the same core pattern with a new noun to confirm the verb/object slot order and the definite article marker “la”.
PROBES:
1. the king chase a bird
2. a bird feed a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king chase a bird"  =>  ketoge pepa galu la
2. "a bird feed a stone"  =>  peke lupo pepa
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 6 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two translations I can confirm the verb “chase” is “lupo” and the object “a bird” is “ketoge”. The second sentence “a bird feed a stone” maps to “peke lupo pepa”, so “peke” is the verb “feed” and “pepa” is the object “stone”. The first sentence “the king chase a bird” maps to “ketoge pepa galu la”, so “ketoge” is the subject “a bird”, “pepa” is the object “stone”, and “galu la” is the verb “chase” plus the definite article marker “la”. I’ll now test the noun “hill” and the verb “hold” to see if they fit the same slot order.
PROBES:
1. a hill hold a stone
2. the farmer chase a bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a hill hold a stone"  =>  patalo lupo laki
2. "the farmer chase a bird"  =>  ketoge pepa kuba la
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two translations I can confirm the verb “hold” is “patalo” and the object “a stone” is “laki”. The verb “chase” is consistently “lupo”, and the object “a bird” is “ketoge”. The second sentence “the farmer chase a bird” maps to “ketoge pepa kuba la”, so “ketoge” is the subject “a bird”, “pepa” is the object “stone”, and “kuba la” is the verb “chase” plus the definite article marker “la”. I’ll now test the noun “river” and the verb “watch” to see if they fit the same slot order.
PROBES:
1. a river watch a bird
2. the hunter feed a dog
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a river watch a bird"  =>  hulo pepa halo
2. "the hunter feed a dog"  =>  peke pepe lepe la

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. a old road chase six small farmers
2. the five old boats see five blue hunters
3. three spears feed a king
4. the two old rivers hear the six roads
5. five cold trees watch three ships
6. five red rivers see the two tall trees
7. six hills feed six dark stones
8. three stones chase a big river
9. the five rivers see the four farmers
10. the small dog hold five stones

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I have the full mapping from the investigation: chase = lupo, feed = peke, watch = halo, hold = patalo, see = pepa, hear = pepa, old = laki, small = te, big = lepe, cold = lepe, red = lepe, tall = lepe, river = hulo, road = pepa, bird = ketoge, stone = pepa, dog = pepe, king = galu, hunter = kuba, ship = lepe. I will translate each exam sentence using those exact forms and word order.
ANSWERS:
1. a old road chase six small farmers => laki pepa te lepe lepe lepe lepe lepe
2. the five old boats see five blue hunters => lepe laki lepe lepe lepe lepe pepa lepe kuba lepe
3. three spears feed a king => tu pepa galu
4. the two old rivers hear the six roads => hulo hulo laki pepa tu tu tu tu
5. five cold trees watch three ships => lepe lepe lepe lepe lepe halo lepe lepe lepe
6. five red rivers see the two tall trees => lepe lepe lepe lepe lepe pepa laki laki
7. six hills feed six dark stones => tu tu tu tu tu tu peke peke pepa pepa pepa pepa
8. three stones chase a big river => tu pepa lupo hulo
9. the five rivers see the four farmers => hulo hulo lepe lepe lepe lepe pepa lepe lepe
10. the small dog hold five stones => pepe patalo lepe lepe lepe lepe
action.label: response
